1: The attribution formula marks the opening of Elihu’s second speech (Job 34–35), which addresses a different angle than the first: where Job 33 focused on the channels of divine communication and the path of restoration, Job 34 argues from the premise of divine justice as the foundation of cosmic order. The transition to a new speech while continuing the same dialogue is a structural feature of the book’s dialogue form; Elihu’s four speeches (Job 32–37) are each introduced by the attribution formula, giving them the same formal status as the three friends’ speeches.
2: “Hear my words, O wise men; listen to me, you who have knowledge” broadens Elihu’s audience from Job alone to the wider assembly of the wise. In ancient Near Eastern contexts, wisdom debates were conducted before an audience of sages who would evaluate the arguments; the appeal to the “wise men” (ḥăkāmîm) and those with “knowledge” (yōdᵉʿîm) as co-judges of the case is a rhetorical strategy that places Elihu’s argument before the recognized authorities of the wisdom tradition. This appeal to the community of sages parallels the Babylonian Theodicy’s structure, where both disputants appeal to the tradition of the wise to evaluate their claims.
3: “The ear tests words as the palate tastes food” is the most memorable of Elihu’s several food-and-speech metaphors. Job had used a similar image in Job 6:30 (“cannot my palate discern falsehood?”) and Job 12:11 (“does not the ear test words as the palate tastes food?”). The image encodes a fundamental principle of ANE epistemology: wisdom is acquired through careful, sensory-like discrimination among competing claims, not through the bare assertion of tradition. The ear that “tests” (bāḥan) words uses a metallurgical term for assaying metal for purity — the same process of discriminating genuine from counterfeit.
4: “Let us choose together what is right; let us determine among ourselves what is good” proposes a communal deliberative process. The verbs bāḥar (choose) and yādaʿ (know/determine) applied to “what is right” and “what is good” are the vocabulary of ethical discernment in wisdom literature. The inclusion of the audience as co-deliberators (“together,” “among ourselves”) is a democratic move within the hierarchical wisdom tradition: rather than simply pronouncing a verdict, Elihu invites communal reasoning. This methodology parallels the ANE practice of the deliberative assembly of elders at the city gate — the same setting in which Job himself once exercised authority (Job 29:7–10).
5: Elihu’s quotation of Job — “I am righteous, and God has denied me my right” — accurately summarizes Job’s central claim, particularly as expressed in Job 27:2 (“as God lives, who has taken away my right”) and Job 19:6–7 (“know that God has overthrown me”). The legal term mišpāṭ (right, judgment, justice) is the same term Job has used throughout: he claims that YHWH has denied him the just legal outcome (mišpāṭ) that his innocence warrants. Elihu identifies this as the precise point of error: the premise that God can deny a person their deserved mišpāṭ contradicts the nature of the divine judge.
6: “Must I lie about my case? My wound is past healing, though I have done no wrong” continues Elihu’s quotation of Job. Job’s claim that his wound (ḥiṣṣî, lit. arrow-wound or wound) is “past healing” (ʾānûš, incurable/desperate) despite his innocence is precisely his theodicy argument: the incurability of his suffering without a corresponding guilt is the empirical evidence of divine injustice. Jeremiah uses the same vocabulary of incurable wound in his laments (Jeremiah 15:18: “why is my wound incurable, refusing to heal?”). Elihu will argue that Job’s error is in the premise: no wound that God inflicts is truly incurable or unjust.
7: “What man is like Job, who drinks mockery like water?” uses the “drinks X like water” idiom to express habitual or natural behavior (cf. Job 15:16: “who drinks wickedness like water”). Water as the most basic and necessary daily drink made it the natural standard for habitual consumption: someone who “drinks” something like water does it effortlessly and constantly. Elihu accuses Job of habitually practicing mockery (lāʿag, scorn/derision) — specifically, derision toward God. The charge reflects Elihu’s evaluation of Job’s speeches as having crossed from legitimate complaint into something closer to contemptuous rejection of divine authority.
8: “Who keeps company with evildoers and walks with wicked men” applies the wisdom tradition’s fundamental social principle: a person is defined by their associations. Proverbs emphasizes this repeatedly (Proverbs 13:20: “walk with the wise and become wise; the companion of fools suffers harm”; Job 22:24–25). The accusation that Job “keeps company” with evildoers is based not on his literal social circle but on the ideological company his arguments keep: by arguing that piety is unrewarded (Job 21:14–15), Job is saying exactly what the wicked say in practice. In wisdom thinking, sharing the argument is equivalent to sharing the company.
9: “A man gains nothing by delighting in God” is Elihu’s sharp paraphrase of Job’s argument in Job 21:14–15 (“the wicked say: what do we gain by praying to him?”). The word rāṣāh (delight in, be pleased with) describes the relationship of genuine religious devotion. Elihu’s point is that Job’s empirical theodicy in Job 21 effectively makes the same claim as the wicked: religious devotion has no demonstrable payoff. Whether Job intends it or not, his argument provides intellectual cover for the wicked’s practical atheism. The adversary’s question in Job 1:9 (“does Job fear God for nothing?”) returns here from a different angle.
10: “Far be it from God to do evil, far from the Almighty to commit wrong” is the formal statement of divine moral perfection that undergirds Elihu’s entire argument. The formula ḥālîlāh lᵉ’ēl mērešaʿ (far be it from God for wickedness) appears elsewhere in similar formulas defending divine rectitude (Genesis 18:25: Abraham’s “far be it from you to do such a thing — to kill the righteous with the wicked”). In ANE theology, the moral character of the deity was assumed to be the foundation of cosmic order; Deuteronomy 32:4 (“a God of faithfulness and without iniquity, just and right is he”) and Psalms 92:15 express the same axiom that Elihu now deploys.
11: “He repays each person according to their work and deals with everyone according to their conduct” is the retribution formula at its most precise. The same formula appears in Psalms 62:12 (“you repay everyone according to their deeds”), Proverbs 24:12 (“does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? Does not he who guards your life know it? Will he not repay everyone according to what they have done?”), and is cited in the NT (Romans 2:6; 2 Corinthians 5:10; Revelation 22:12). In ANE legal contexts, the principle of proportional recompense (lex talionis and its broader application) was the foundation of justice; Elihu applies this to divine governance to establish that God cannot act unjustly by his own nature.
12: “Of a truth, God will not act wickedly; the Almighty will not pervert justice” states the negative of v10–11: not only is God morally perfect, but the specific charge of “perverting justice” (ʿāwat mišpāṭ) is impossible for him. Job had used the same phrase in Job 8:3 (quoting Bildad) and in 19:6 where he charged that God had “perverted” his case. The impossibility of divine justice-perversion is the theological axiom that makes Job’s entire legal complaint against God self-defeating: one cannot charge the judge of all the earth with injustice, because the judge’s just character is the precondition of justice existing at all.
13: “Who gave him his charge over the earth? Who appointed him over the whole world?” argues for divine sovereignty on the grounds that God has no superior who appointed him. In ancient Near Eastern royal ideology, kings were appointed by the gods; the greatest kings were “chosen by Marduk” or “called by Enlil.” Even the divine king had a divine appointing authority. But God himself has no such superior: he rules the earth not by appointment from a higher authority but by inherent sovereignty. The rhetorical questions expect the answer “no one” — God’s accountability structure is vertical, not horizontal: he answers to no peer or superior.
14: “If he should set his heart against us and gather to himself his spirit and breath” introduces the argument from divine sustenance: all life exists because God continuously provides it. The “setting his heart against us” uses the idiom of directed intention (“set his heart against” = attend to with hostile purpose). In ANE cosmology, the withdrawal of divine sustaining power was one of the primary mechanisms of world destruction: the Mesopotamian flood narratives (Atrahasis, Gilgamesh) describe the gods deciding to withdraw life from humanity; Psalms 104:29 uses the same image (“when you hide your face, they are troubled; when you take away their breath, they die and return to their dust”).
15: “All flesh would perish together, and human beings would return to dust” is the direct consequence of divine breath-withdrawal. The “return to dust” (yāšubu ʿal-ʿāpār) echoes Genesis 3:19 (“you are dust, and to dust you shall return”) and expresses the creaturely dependence on divine breath for the continuation of life. The argument’s logic is a reductio: if God is the source of all life, then Job’s complaint that God is unjust requires that the only truly just universe would be one with no life at all — since all life exists at God’s discretion. The universal creaturely dependence on divine breath establishes God’s unchallenged sovereignty over life and death.
16: The second attention-call — “if you have understanding, hear this; listen to what I am saying” — marks a structural division within the speech. Elihu has established the theological axioms (vv10–15) and now turns to their application to Job’s case (vv16–37). The conditional “if you have understanding” (’im-bînāh) is a wisdom challenge: only a person with genuine bînāh (discernment, understanding) will be able to hear what follows. In the wisdom tradition, bînāh was a special gift that distinguished the sage from the fool (Proverbs 2:2–6); the implicit suggestion is that Job’s failure to hear God has been a failure of understanding rather than a failure of divine communication.
17: “Shall one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn the One who is righteous and mighty?” deploys the argument from governance: a ruler who hates justice cannot maintain order, and the evidence of cosmic order proves that the cosmic ruler does not hate justice. The rhetorical question inverts Job’s complaint: Job has been trying to condemn God; Elihu asks whether it is logical to condemn the only being whose justice is the precondition of all other justice. In ANE political theology, the king who “hated justice” was by definition a tyrant; Hammurabi’s code prologue famously declares that the king was appointed “to bring about justice in the land, to destroy the wicked and evil, to prevent the strong oppressing the weak.”
18: “Is it right to say to a king, ‘You are worthless,’ and to nobles, ‘You are wicked’?” employs the ancient legal concept of lèse-majesté — the crime of insulting royal dignity. In the ancient Near East, insulting a king was a capital offense; the Mesopotamian law codes and biblical texts both treat royal dignity as protected by law (cf. Ecclesiastes 10:20: “do not curse the king, even in your thought”; Acts 23:5: “you shall not speak evil of a ruler of your people”). If it is wrong to insult a mortal king, Elihu argues a fortiori that it is far worse to speak as Job has about the divine King.
19: “How much less to him who shows no partiality to princes and does not favor the rich over the poor, for they are all the work of his hands” develops the a fortiori argument by showing that the divine King is uniquely qualified for the divine role: he is perfectly impartial. Partiality in judgment (nāśāʾ pānîm, lit. lifting of the face) was a recognized legal failure (Deuteronomy 1:17; Job 16:19; Leviticus 19:15). Ancient codes repeatedly emphasized the obligation of impartiality; Hammurabi’s code opens with the god Anu and Enlil appointing Hammurabi to enforce justice with impartiality. God’s impartiality is grounded in creaturely equality: “they are all the work of his hands” removes the social distinctions that create partiality.
20: “In a moment they die; at midnight the people are convulsed and pass away, and the mighty are removed — without human hand” describes the suddenness of divine judgment. The nocturnal divine action — “at midnight” — echoes the Passover night where the firstborn were struck (Exodus 12:29: “at midnight YHWH struck down all the firstborn”). The phrase “without human hand” (bᵉlō’ yad) is the language of miraculous or direct divine action that requires no human agency (cf. Daniel 2:34,45 where the stone that strikes the statue was “cut out, not by human hands”). Divine judgment is both sudden and entirely self-executing.
21: “His eyes are on the ways of everyone, and he sees all their steps” states the divine omniscience doctrine that grounds Elihu’s subsequent argument. The “eyes of YHWH” as a symbol of universal surveillance appears throughout the OT (2 Chronicles 16:9: “the eyes of YHWH range throughout the earth”; Proverbs 15:3: “the eyes of YHWH are in every place, watching the evil and the good”; Psalms 33:13–15). In Mesopotamian theology, the sun-god Shamash was understood as the god who saw all human deeds and was therefore the patron of justice; the “all-seeing god” concept was fundamental to ANE theologies of divine governance.
22: “There is no darkness or deep shadow where evildoers can hide themselves” applies the omniscience doctrine to the impossibility of escaping divine judgment. The darkness/shadow (ḥōšek and ṣalmāwet) that evildoers might use to conceal themselves is precisely the darkness-of-death imagery that pervades Job’s own description of his situation (Job 3:5; Job 10:21–22). Psalms 139:7–12 develops this theme most fully: “even the darkness is not dark to you; the night is bright as the day, for darkness is as light with you.” The ANE concept of the sun-god who sees through darkness undergirds this theology.
23: “He does not impose on anyone more than is warranted to bring them before God in judgment” addresses the proportionality of divine judgment. In ancient legal systems, the principle of proportional punishment was enshrined in law codes: Hammurabi’s code specified that punishment should match the crime precisely. The claim that God does not impose more than warranted is Elihu’s implicit defense against Job’s accusation that his suffering exceeds any conceivable sin. Elihu insists the suffering is precisely calibrated to bring Job to the divine court — not as a victim but as a defendant whose case will be justly adjudicated.
24: “He shatters the mighty without investigation and sets others in their place” describes divine sovereign action that requires no judicial inquiry because the divine omniscience already knows the facts. Human courts required investigation (ḥēqer, inquiry/examination) because human judges have limited knowledge; divine courts require no such process because God’s knowledge is immediate and complete. This is the theological basis for the divine action in v20 (“without human hand”): the same divine omniscience that makes investigation unnecessary makes execution immediate. The “sets others in their place” reflects the ANE concept of providential succession: God removes unjust rulers and installs others.
25: “For he knows their deeds, and he overturns them in the night; they are crushed” combines the omniscience argument (v21) with the nocturnal judgment imagery (v20). The verb hāpak (overturns) is the same word used for the divine overthrow of Sodom (Genesis 19:25,29). The “crushing” of the mighty in the night is both sudden and complete: the image is of buildings or threshing that is done in darkness when no observer expects it. The pattern of hidden, sudden, overwhelming divine action is presented as the characteristic mode of divine judgment against the powerful.
26: “He strikes them for their wickedness in an open place where others can see” shifts from the hidden nocturnal judgment to public execution. The two modes of divine judgment — secret nocturnal overthrow (v25) and public open-air punishment (v26) — cover the full range of divine judicial action. In ancient Near Eastern law, public punishment was specifically designed to be witnessed: the stele of Hammurabi was placed in a public space for all to see; executions were public events. The divine open-air striking “where others can see” serves the same deterrent and communal-witness function as public human punishment.
27: “Because they turned away from following him and gave no thought to any of his ways” identifies the root cause of the divine judgment described in vv24–26: apostasy and cognitive/practical disregard. “Turned away from following him” (sāru mēʾaḥărāyw) is the covenantal vocabulary of apostasy; the same language describes Israel’s defection throughout Deuteronomy and the prophets. “Gave no thought to his ways” (lō’ śākᵉlû, not perceiving/attending to) is the practical inattention to divine instruction that results in practical atheism even without formal theological rejection.
28: “So that they brought the cry of the poor before him, and he heard the outcry of the afflicted” identifies the oppression of the poor as the specific trigger for divine judgment. In ANE royal ideology, the protection of the poor and weak was the primary duty of the just king. Hammurabi’s famous prologue declares: “so that the strong might not oppress the weak; to care for the widow and orphan.” The divine response to the cry of the poor (ze’aqat ʿănāwîm) is a consistent OT theme: Exodus 3:7 (“I have heard the cry of my people”); Psalms 9:12 (“he does not forget the cry of the afflicted”). The judgment against the mighty in vv24–27 is presented here as the direct result of this divine hearing.
29: “When he is quiet, who can condemn? When he hides his face, who can see him?” is Elihu’s answer to Job’s repeated complaint about divine silence and hiddenness. Job had said in Job 23:3–5 (“if only I knew where to find him... I would lay out my case before him”) and Job 13:24 (“why do you hide your face?”). Elihu’s point is that divine silence and divine hiddenness do not constitute injustice: the judge who is quiet cannot be condemned; the judge who hides his face is not absent but attending to his purposes. The divine freedom to be silent is an aspect of sovereignty that is not subject to human legal challenge.
30: “Lest a godless person reign and ensnare the people” states the providential purpose of divine intervention in national governance. The “godless person” (ḥānēf) who reigns is the classic ANE tyrant figure — the ruler who governs without reference to divine justice and therefore exploits rather than protects the people. The verb “ensnare” (môqēšê’ām) uses hunting-net imagery for the tyrant’s capture of his own citizens. Divine providential removal of such rulers — the pattern described in vv24–25 — is thus not arbitrary power but the protection of the social order from the worst possible governance.
31: “Has anyone said to God: I have endured my punishment; I will not sin any more?” introduces the penitential formula as the correct response to divine discipline. Elihu is proposing the model response that Job has conspicuously refused to give: a confession of sin, an acceptance of punishment as just, and a commitment to future amendment. This three-part penitential structure — acknowledgment of punishment, confession of sin, pledge of reform — is exactly the structure of the Mesopotamian penitential prayers (šuilla), which regularly include: “I have sinned; my punishment is just; I will not sin again.” Job’s refusal to make this response is, in Elihu’s view, the primary theological error of his speeches.
32: “Teach me what I cannot see; if I have done wrong, I will not do it again” continues the ideal penitential response. The request “teach me what I cannot see” acknowledges epistemic limitation: the penitent recognizes that there may be unperceived sin requiring instruction. This humility before divine knowledge was the starting point of the Mesopotamian penitential tradition: the šuilla prayers acknowledge “the sin I have committed without knowing” (lā idû). The pledge “I will not do it again” is the commitment to amendment that completes the three-part structure. Elihu is essentially teaching Job the script of the model penitent, which Job’s own speeches have dramatically refused to follow.
33: “Should he repay you on your own terms because you reject his? For you must choose, not I; tell me then what you know” poses the fundamental question of Elihu’s second speech. Job has been insisting that God operate by the terms Job sets — the terms of a human legal court where defendants can demand explanations and challenge verdicts. Elihu asks whether God is obligated to accept the framework Job proposes simply because Job refuses to accept God’s framework. The rhetorical challenge “you must choose, not I” places the decision back with Job: which framework will govern this discussion?
34: “People of understanding will agree with me, and every wise person who hears me will say” appeals again to the community of the wise as co-judges. In ANE wisdom debates, the verdict of the broader wisdom community was the court of final appeal: not a legal verdict but the consensus of recognized sages. Elihu’s confidence that the wise will endorse his argument reflects the debater’s standard rhetorical move of pre-emptively claiming the audience’s support. The indirect structure — “they will say” rather than “I say” — lends the verdict the authority of communal rather than merely personal judgment.
35: “Job speaks without knowledge; his words are without insight” is the community of the wise’s verdict as Elihu presents it. The charge of speaking “without knowledge” (bᵉlōʾ daʿat) is among the most serious that the wisdom tradition can level: it is the charge that YHWH himself will make in Job 38:2 (“who is this that obscures my counsel with words without knowledge?”). In the wisdom framework, speaking without knowledge is not merely an intellectual failure but a moral one: the person who claims more than they know violates the epistemological humility that wisdom requires. Elihu’s use of this charge anticipates and aligns with the divine verdict.
36: “Would that Job were tested to the limit, because his answers are like those of wicked men” is Elihu’s harshest statement against Job. The wish that Job be tested “to the limit” (ʿad-neṣaḥ, forever/to the extreme) may strike the reader as vindictive, but in context it reflects the wisdom principle that a person’s true character is revealed under maximum pressure. The book’s prologue established that Job’s suffering was precisely this kind of test; Elihu, without knowing the prologue’s backstory, advocates for the continuation of the very process that is already underway. The irony is that YHWH’s verdict in 42:7 will declare that Job, not Elihu, spoke what was right — the very opposite of Elihu’s verdict here.
37: “For he adds rebellion to his sin; he mocks us and heaps up words against God” is Elihu’s final charge against Job in this speech. The accusation of “rebellion” (pešaʿ) in addition to sin is a gradation: pešaʿ is the strongest word for transgression in the OT, often used of political rebellion against a sovereign (cf. 1 Kings 12:19; 2 Kings 1:1). The charge that Job mocks the sages (“us”) and heaps up words against God is the summary of everything Elihu has been building toward: Job’s speeches are not merely mistaken but constitute an act of revolt against the divine sovereign. YHWH’s own response from the whirlwind will suggest a more complex and compassionate judgment.
